message:
chore(beads): queue ext4 ioctl and btrfs FIEMAP operator-path coverage beads

Capture five new/changed parity beads that surfaced during the
ongoing ioctl and FIEMAP coverage sweep so br ready mirrors the
planned work:

Opened (operator-path and kernel-reference coverage gaps):
- bd-4hzfa: add mounted-path coverage for ext4
  FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY v1 and the ENODATA path — existing
  unit coverage doesn't exercise the full FUSE-mounted ioctl surface.
- bd-7xzs7: add kernel-reference and operator-path coverage for
  ext4 POSIX ACL xattrs (system.posix_acl_access /
  system.posix_acl_default).
- bd-8vll5: add btrfs FIEMAP edge-case coverage for inline and
  prealloc extents, which the current parity harness only hits on
  regular extents.
- bd-k1r7x: revalidate the stale xfstests ioctl allowlist after the
  FIEMAP and SETFLAGS coverage landings — several entries are now
  covered and can be rolled into the green set.

In progress:
- bd-devbv: add mounted-path E2E and ioctl-trace coverage for ext4
  EXT4_IOC_GETVERSION, picked up while landing the MOVE_EXT donor
  validation.

Pure issue-tracker bookkeeping — no source code changes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

+{"id":"bd-4hzfa","title":"add mounted-path coverage for ext4 FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY v1 and ENODATA paths","description":"Survey finding: old fscrypt policy lookup is implemented in `ffs-core` and dispatched in `ffs-fuse`, with unit tests for success, short-buffer rejection, and `ENODATA`, but there is no mounted-path proof in `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs`.

Scope:
- Add mounted-path coverage for `FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY` against an ext4 image that advertises `ENCRYPT` and carries a legacy v1 fscrypt context.
- Add a mounted-path absence case that proves stable `ENODATA` behavior for an unencrypted inode or missing context.
- Assert ioctl-trace behavior so transport-layer `ENOTTY`/`EOPNOTSUPP` skips remain distinguishable from FrankenFS regressions.
- Update `FEATURE_PARITY.md` wording once operator-visible coverage exists.

Acceptance:
- Success and missing-policy cases are both covered through the real mounted path.
- The test output makes v1-only behavior explicit and auditable.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T03:16:17.346541483Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T03:16:17.346541483Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ext4","fscrypt","fuse","ioctl","testing"]}

 {"id":"bd-4ogh","title":"Implement ext4/btrfs parity features: decompression, inline data, bigalloc, ZERO_RANGE","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-22T18:28:47.537528441Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T18:29:02.634096851Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T18:29:02.633525312Z","close_reason":"Implemented 4 new parity features:
(1) btrfs transparent decompression: ZLIB (flate2) + ZSTD in read path for inline and regular extents. Added ram_bytes field to BtrfsExtentData enum. LZO deferred (custom framing).
(2) ext4 inline data read: reads from inode extent_bytes + xattr_ibody when EXT4_INLINE_DATA_FL is set. Removed INLINE_DATA from REJECTED_V1 incompat set.
(3) ext4 bigalloc infrastructure: added cluster_ratio to FsGeometry (cluster_size/block_size for BIGALLOC, else 1).
(4) ext4 ZERO_RANGE fallocate: writes zero blocks to existing allocated extents in range. Mode 0x10 now accepted.
All tests pass (471 ffs-core, 360 ffs-ondisk, 142 ffs-alloc, 117 ffs-btrfs, 114 ffs-extent).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","ext4","parity"]}

+{"id":"bd-7xzs7","title":"add kernel-reference and operator-path coverage for ext4 POSIX ACL xattrs","description":"Survey finding: `ffs-xattr` parses and lists `system.posix_acl_access` / `system.posix_acl_default`, and README claims `system.*` coverage for POSIX ACLs, but the harness coverage is still concentrated on `user.*` plus `security.selinux`. There is no kernel-reference or mounted-path proof for ACL namespace behavior.

Scope:
- Extend `crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs` to create ACL xattrs through `debugfs` and compare list/get behavior against FrankenFS.
- Add operator-path coverage for ACL xattrs where the environment has the needed privilege, with deterministic soft-skip behavior when `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` is unavailable.
- Cover both access and default ACL names, plus at least one malformed or missing-value edge case.
- Update parity/docs wording so ACL support claims match tested surface exactly.

Acceptance:
- ACL namespace behavior is proven against kernel tooling, not only crate-local parsing tests.
- Mounted-path coverage or explicit privileged soft-skip evidence exists for the operator-visible path.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T03:16:25.632276975Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T03:16:25.632276975Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["acl","ext4","fuse","testing","xattr"]}

 {"id":"bd-820","title":"Implement RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush","description":"# Implement RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush

## GOAL
Ensure repair symbols are regenerated when block groups are modified, as mandated by spec §0.4: "A block write that never triggers repair symbol refresh for its group is a spec-conformance bug."

## BACKGROUND
Currently repair symbols are generated at image creation. When blocks are modified:
1. Group becomes "stale" (symbols no longer match data)
2. Must regenerate before corruption can be recovered
3. Two modes: lazy (on next scrub) or eager (on fsync)

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Dirty Group Tracking
```rust
pub struct DirtyGroupTracker {
    dirty: BitVec,  // One bit per group
}

impl DirtyGroupTracker {
    pub fn mark_dirty(&mut self, group: GroupNumber);
    pub fn is_dirty(&self, group: GroupNumber) -> bool;
    pub fn clear(&mut self, group: GroupNumber);
    pub fn dirty_groups(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = GroupNumber>;
}
```

### 2. Refresh Triggers
- **Lazy**: Background scrub regenerates symbols for dirty groups
- **Eager (fsync)**: Regenerate immediately for committed groups
- **Configurable**: MountConfig.repair_refresh_policy

### 3. Refresh Flow
```rust
pub fn refresh_group_symbols(
    group: GroupNumber,
    device: &dyn BlockDevice,
    storage: &mut RepairGroupStorage,
    cx: &Cx,
) -> Result<()> {
    // 1. Read all blocks in group
    // 2. Regenerate RaptorQ symbols
    // 3. Write to repair storage slots
    // 4. Clear dirty flag
}
```

### 4. Budget Integration
- Refresh respects Cx budget
- Can be interrupted and resumed
- Progress tracked per-group

## TESTS
1. Unit: Dirty tracking set/clear
2. Integration: Write marks group dirty
3. Integration: Scrub refreshes dirty group
4. Integration: fsync refreshes if eager mode
5. E2E: Corruption after refresh -> recovery works

## LOGGING
- Mark dirty (trace): group number, cause
- Refresh start (debug): group, block count
- Refresh complete (info): group, duration, symbol count

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Every modified group eventually refreshed
2. Lazy mode: refreshed within scrub interval
3. Eager mode: refreshed on fsync
4. Recovery works for refreshed groups","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:54:43.407560046Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T14:08:46.778165832Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T14:08:46.778143631Z","close_reason":"Implemented dirty-group refresh APIs (mark/query/flush), aligned refresh logging, and added flush-path + tracker tests in ffs-repair pipeline; validated via rch cargo test/check/clippy and cargo fmt --check.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cow","raptorq","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-820","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:20.818428330Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-820","depends_on_id":"bd-22w.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:51:49.899554131Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":191,"issue_id":"bd-820","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

Cluster: repair_durability
Mapped graveyard sections: 1.1 RaptorQ + 12.13 Change-point + 11.11 PoR
EV score: 25 (Impact*Confidence*Reuse / (Effort*Friction))
Priority tier: S/A
Budgeted mode: decode_budget_ms=200, io_budget_mb=64, max_repair_attempts=3
Fallback trigger: read-only degrade + evidence ledger + manual repair workflow

Required proof artifacts for execution on this bead:
- Baseline + hotspot evidence before changes.
- Isomorphism proof block per lever.
- Evidence-ledger event for decision path + fallback invocation.
- Repro pack: env.json, manifest.json, repro.lock (and LEGAL.md if IP risk appears).","created_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:30Z"}]}
@@ -497,6 +499,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-8rgp","title":"Add btrfs write-path FUSE E2E tests mirroring ext4 coverage","description":"## What
Add btrfs write-path FUSE E2E tests that exercise create/write/mkdir/rename/unlink/truncate/
xattr/fallocate/symlink through the actual FUSE mount interface, mirroring the ext4 FUSE E2E
tests that already exist in fuse_e2e.rs.

## Why
ext4 has comprehensive FUSE E2E tests: fuse_create_and_read_file, fuse_write_overwrite_and_append,
fuse_mkdir_and_nested_create, fuse_unlink_removes_file, fuse_rename_file, fuse_hard_link,
fuse_symlink_create_and_follow, fuse_setattr_truncate — all exercising real kernel VFS through
FUSE mount.

btrfs write operations are tested only at the unit level (OpenFs methods in ffs-core) and in
the crash matrix. There are NO btrfs FUSE E2E write tests that go through the kernel.

## What Already Exists
- ext4 FUSE E2E write tests in crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs (lines 531-673)
- btrfs FUSE E2E read tests exist (mount + read files)
- Helper: with_rw_mount() for ext4 RW FUSE mounts
- btrfs mkfs.btrfs image creation in fuse_e2e.rs (line 1889-1910)
- btrfs unit write tests in ffs-core (create, mkdir, rename, fallocate, etc.)

## Implementation Plan
Add to crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs, using a with_btrfs_rw_mount() helper:

fn fuse_btrfs_create_and_read_file()
  - mkfs.btrfs → mount --rw → create file → read back → verify content → unmount

fn fuse_btrfs_write_and_read_back()
  - mount → create → write 4KB → read → verify → unmount

fn fuse_btrfs_mkdir_and_nested_create()
  - mount → mkdir → create file in subdir → read → verify → unmount

fn fuse_btrfs_rename_file()
  - mount → create → rename → verify new path exists, old doesn't → unmount

fn fuse_btrfs_unlink_removes_file()
  - mount → create → unlink → verify gone → unmount

fn fuse_btrfs_setattr_truncate()
  - mount → create → write 8KB → truncate to 4KB → verify size → unmount

fn fuse_btrfs_xattr_set_get_list_remove()
  - mount → create file → setxattr → getxattr → listxattr → removexattr → unmount

fn fuse_btrfs_fallocate_prealloc()
  - mount → create → fallocate 4096 bytes → verify size → unmount

fn fuse_btrfs_symlink_create_and_follow()
  - mount → create target → symlink → readlink → read through symlink → unmount

### Helper: with_btrfs_rw_mount()
- Create 256MB sparse file
- mkfs.btrfs -f on it
- Mount via FrankenFS FUSE with --rw
- Run test closure
- Unmount
- Skip if mkfs.btrfs or /dev/fuse unavailable

## Considerations
- Tests need both mkfs.btrfs and /dev/fuse — skip gracefully if either missing
- btrfs minimum image ~128-256MB (much larger than ext4 8MB) — use sparse files
- Use --test-threads=1 for FUSE tests to avoid mount contention
- Tests must unmount cleanly even on assertion failure (use Drop guard)
- btrfs write support is experimental — some operations may not be fully wired
  through FUSE yet; tests should document any that return EOPNOTSUPP

## Test Plan
- cargo test -p ffs-harness --test fuse_e2e -- fuse_btrfs --nocapture --test-threads=1
- All tests pass when mkfs.btrfs and /dev/fuse available
- All tests skip cleanly when either is unavailable
- No interference with ext4 FUSE tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 9 btrfs FUSE E2E write-path tests implemented
- [ ] with_btrfs_rw_mount() helper handles setup/teardown
- [ ] Tests skip gracefully without mkfs.btrfs or /dev/fuse
- [ ] All 9 tests pass on systems with FUSE+btrfs-progs
- [ ] Unmount cleanup is reliable (Drop guard)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:46:46.038588133Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T21:23:42.513062175Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T21:23:42.512793045Z","close_reason":"Already implemented: 19 btrfs FUSE E2E tests exist at fuse_e2e.rs:1965-2395 including create, mkdir, rename, unlink, symlink, xattr, fsync, large write","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-8s71","title":"Add backpressure shed checks to unlink/rmdir/rename/link FUSE handlers","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-01T20:43:04.029554734Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T20:45:29.983042677Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T20:45:29.982765542Z","close_reason":"Already implemented: unlink/link have inline should_shed checks; rmdir/rename use enforce_mutation_guards via dispatch helpers. All paths covered.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-8tr","title":"ext4: Implement inode location math helper (inode -> table block + offset)","description":"Goal: given (inode_number, superblock fields, group desc), compute where the inode bytes live on disk.

Deliverables:
- Pure helper: inode_number -> (group, index_in_group).
- Use group desc inode_table pointer and inode_size to compute:
  - table_start_block
  - inode_byte_offset_from_table_start
  - absolute byte offset on device
- Handle ext4 inode numbering rules (inode numbers start at 1; 0 invalid).

Acceptance:
- Unit tests for boundary inodes: 1, inodes_per_group, inodes_per_group+1, last inode.
- Overflow/invalid inputs produce ParseError/FfsError (no panics).
- Helper is used by higher-level inode read code (ffs-inode later).","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"AzureBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:17:15.392537885Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:37:56.483745546Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T17:37:56.483718756Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ext4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-8tr","depends_on_id":"bd-2w9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:18:06.687704093Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-8vll5","title":"add btrfs FIEMAP edge-case coverage for inline and prealloc extents","description":"Survey finding: mounted-path btrfs FIEMAP coverage currently exercises only a plain regular file. In `ffs-core`, `btrfs_fiemap()` has special-case behavior for inline extents (`physical=0`, `FIEMAP_EXTENT_LAST`) and prealloc extents (`FIEMAP_EXTENT_UNWRITTEN`), but those branches are not proven by harness coverage.

Scope:
- Add deterministic unit/conformance coverage for inline and prealloc btrfs extent items through `OpenFs::fiemap` or the closest stable lower-level seam.
- Where practical, extend mounted-path FUSE coverage to a real btrfs image that hits at least one non-plain extent shape, with soft-skip rules if the environment cannot create it deterministically.
- Assert logical offsets, physical offset conventions, and `FIEMAP_EXTENT_LAST` / `FIEMAP_EXTENT_UNWRITTEN` flag behavior.
- Update parity notes if the operator-visible claim needs qualification for these extent shapes.

Acceptance:
- Inline and prealloc btrfs FIEMAP branches are covered by deterministic tests.
- The test matrix makes it clear which btrfs extent shapes are proven only at unit/conformance level versus mounted path.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T03:16:43.215379850Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T03:16:43.215379850Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","fiemap","fuse","ioctl","testing"]}

Map explicit worker_threads values into mount options via max_background and congestion_threshold so it changes kernel-side request queue behavior.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:24:30.145734334Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T19:25:24.881117003Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T19:25:24.820870847Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuse","mount","performance"],"comments":[{"id":291,"issue_id":"bd-907l","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Mapped explicit MountOptions.worker_threads to kernel FUSE queue tuning options in build_mount_options: max_background=<threads> and congestion_threshold=<3/4 threads>. Added tests build_mount_options_includes_queue_tuning_when_worker_threads_explicit and build_mount_options_auto_worker_threads_omits_queue_tuning. Validation: cargo fmt; cargo fmt --check; targeted new tests; cargo check/clippy -p ffs-fuse --all-targets; cargo test -p ffs-fuse (full crate) all pass.","created_at":"2026-02-22T19:25:24Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-jtt2","title":"Implement external journal paired replay (not just UUID validation)","description":"BACKGROUND: ext4 filesystems can use an external journal device (JOURNAL_DEV incompat flag on the data FS, journal_dev field points to device number, journal_uuid links the pair). When crash recovery is needed, the JBD2 journal blocks live on the external device rather than in an internal inode.

CURRENT STATE: OpenOptions::external_journal_path accepts an external journal image path. maybe_probe_external_journal opens it, reads its JBD2 superblock, validates UUID pairing and block size match, and stores ExternalJournalInfo on OpenFs. But maybe_replay_ext4_journal short-circuits when journal_dev != 0 — it logs 'journal replay skipped' and returns Ok(None). No actual replay occurs.

The log message honestly states: 'external journal UUID validation only; full paired replay not yet implemented'.

IMPLEMENTATION PLAN:
1. When journal_dev != 0 AND external_journal_path is Some AND UUIDs match:
   a. Open the external journal image as a ByteDevice.
   b. Parse the JBD2 superblock from the external device.
   c. Compute journal segments: the entire external device (minus superblock) is the journal area.
   d. Feed the segments to replay_jbd2_segments the same way we do for internal journals.
   e. The replay writes go to the DATA filesystem's device, not the journal device.
2. When journal_dev != 0 AND external_journal_path is None AND crash recovery IS needed:
   - Currently returns UnsupportedFeature error (good — fail-fast).
   - No change needed here.
3. When journal_dev != 0 AND external_journal_path is None AND crash recovery NOT needed:
   - Currently returns Ok(None) with info log (good — graceful skip).
   - No change needed here.

KEY CONSIDERATIONS:
- The external journal's block layout is different from an internal journal. The JBD2 superblock is at block 0 of the device, and journal blocks start immediately after. There's no inode or extent mapping.
- We need to compute JournalSegment { start, blocks } for the external device based on the JBD2 superblock's s_maxlen field.
- The replay must write to the data device, not the journal device. The existing replay infrastructure takes a BlockDevice for writes — we just need to pass the data device.
- After successful replay, we should clear the needs_recovery flag on the data device's superblock.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- A test creates a synthetic data FS + external journal pair, dirties the journal, and verifies replay applies blocks to the data device.
- The log message changes from 'UUID validation only' to 'external journal replay completed'.
- UUID mismatch still fails fast.
- Block size mismatch still fails fast.
- ExternalJournalInfo is populated with journal metadata after successful replay.
- The 'required crash recovery' error path still works when no external path is provided.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:40.642853327Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:42:00.763994355Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:42:00.763527321Z","close_reason":"All subtasks complete. External journal paired replay is fully implemented: UUID validation, segment computation (bd-vdk6), and replay wiring (bd-vql1) are all done. 4 tests covering success path, UUID mismatch, dirty FS requirement, and clean FS skip.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ext4","journal","journal_dev"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-jtt2","depends_on_id":"bd-vdk6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:55.294301302Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-jtt2","depends_on_id":"bd-vql1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:56.821268230Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-spik","title":"Implement 6 parity features: btrfs decompression, ext4 inline/indirect/bigalloc/ZERO_RANGE, btrfs subvol","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-22T22:57:08.050698719Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T22:57:23.369345832Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T22:57:23.368707223Z","close_reason":"Full parity session: implemented 6 new filesystem features to close gaps between FrankenFS and real ext4/btrfs images:

1. btrfs transparent decompression (ZLIB + ZSTD) — compressed extents now readable
2. ext4 inline data read — EXT4_INLINE_DATA_FL files now readable from inode body + xattr
3. ext4 indirect block addressing — pre-extent inodes with direct/indirect/double/triple pointers
4. ext4 bigalloc infrastructure — cluster_ratio field in FsGeometry
5. ext4 FALLOC_FL_ZERO_RANGE — new fallocate mode for zeroing allocated ranges
6. btrfs subvolume mount — configurable subvol_objectid in BtrfsContext

Also fixed during review: compressed extent size overflow, decompressed length validation, inline data xattr parsing (use parse_ibody_xattrs instead of naive offset skip).

Updated FEATURE_PARITY.md: 80/80 tracked features (was 75/75).
473 ffs-core tests pass. All leaf crates clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["parity"]}
 {"id":"bd-sqtrq","title":"stress fuse inode lock ordering under concurrent requests","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:34:13.423718762Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T01:22:03.963392029Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T01:22:03.960982426Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"bd-sv1b2","title":"btrfs core: resolve named subvolume and snapshot mount selections","description":"Survey finding: `FEATURE_PARITY.md` and `README.md` both claim btrfs subvolume mount support, but `OpenFs::from_device` still hardcodes `BTRFS_FS_TREE_OBJECTID` and the operator-facing CLI rejects `--subvol/--snapshot`. This bead owns the core selection logic in `ffs-core`.

Scope:
- Extend `OpenOptions` with an explicit btrfs mount selection contract (default root, named subvolume, named snapshot, and optionally numeric objectid if that simplifies diagnostics).
- Walk the root tree and resolve the requested selection to the mounted `subvol_objectid` plus `subvol_root_dirid`.
- Return deterministic `FfsError` variants for missing selections, ambiguous names, and selection/type mismatches.
- Keep default-root behavior unchanged when no selection is requested.

Acceptance:
- `OpenFs::open_with_options` can mount a non-default subvolume/snapshot without post-open mutation hacks.
- Synthetic and/or kernel-reference tests prove correct resolution for default root, named subvolume, named snapshot, and missing target cases.
- Structured diagnostics include the requested selector and resolved objectid/root dirid.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ubuntu","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","mount","parity"]}
+{"id":"bd-sv2c3","title":"ffs-cli: wire btrfs --subvol and --snapshot mount flags to OpenOptions","description":"Survey finding: `ffs-cli` advertises `--subvol`/`--snapshot` on `mount`, but `validate_btrfs_mount_selection()` hard-rejects both flags even though the underlying codebase already has internal btrfs subvolume context machinery. This bead owns the operator-facing CLI plumbing.

Scope:
- Remove the unconditional rejection path in `crates/ffs-cli/src/main.rs`.
- Thread the requested selection into `OpenOptions` and preserve all existing ext4 behavior.
- Emit structured startup/rejection logs that show requested selector, resolved selection type, and failure class.
- Replace the current `mount_rejects_subvol_flag` / `mount_rejects_snapshot_flag` tests with positive routing tests and deterministic failure diagnostics for nonexistent selectors.

Acceptance:
- `ffs mount ... --subvol <name>` and `ffs mount ... --snapshot <name>` reach `OpenFs::open_with_options` instead of bailing in argument validation.
- Missing selectors fail with clear, stable user-facing errors.

-{"id":"bd-sv1b2","title":"btrfs core: resolve named subvolume and snapshot mount selections","description":"Survey finding: `FEATURE_PARITY.md` and `README.md` both claim btrfs subvolume mount support, but `OpenFs::from_device` still hardcodes `BTRFS_FS_TREE_OBJECTID` and the operator-facing CLI rejects `--subvol/--snapshot`. This bead owns the core selection logic in `ffs-core`.

Scope:
- Extend `OpenOptions` with an explicit btrfs mount selection contract (default root, named subvolume, named snapshot, and optionally numeric objectid if that simplifies diagnostics).
- Walk the root tree and resolve the requested selection to the mounted `subvol_objectid` plus `subvol_root_dirid`.
- Return deterministic `FfsError` variants for missing selections, ambiguous names, and selection/type mismatches.
- Keep default-root behavior unchanged when no selection is requested.

Acceptance:
- `OpenFs::open_with_options` can mount a non-default subvolume/snapshot without post-open mutation hacks.
- Synthetic and/or kernel-reference tests prove correct resolution for default root, named subvolume, named snapshot, and missing target cases.
- Structured diagnostics include the requested selector and resolved objectid/root dirid.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ubuntu","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","mount","parity"]}
-{"id":"bd-sv2c3","title":"ffs-cli: wire btrfs --subvol and --snapshot mount flags to OpenOptions","description":"Survey finding: `ffs-cli` advertises `--subvol`/`--snapshot` on `mount`, but `validate_btrfs_mount_selection()` hard-rejects both flags even though the underlying codebase already has internal btrfs subvolume context machinery. This bead owns the operator-facing CLI plumbing.

Scope:
- Remove the unconditional rejection path in `crates/ffs-cli/src/main.rs`.
- Thread the requested selection into `OpenOptions` and preserve all existing ext4 behavior.
- Emit structured startup/rejection logs that show requested selector, resolved selection type, and failure class.
- Replace the current `mount_rejects_subvol_flag` / `mount_rejects_snapshot_flag` tests with positive routing tests and deterministic failure diagnostics for nonexistent selectors.

Acceptance:
- `ffs mount ... --subvol <name>` and `ffs mount ... --snapshot <name>` reach `OpenFs::open_with_options` instead of bailing in argument validation.
- Missing selectors fail with clear, stable user-facing errors.
- CLI tests cover default-root, explicit subvolume, explicit snapshot, and nonexistent selection cases.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","cli","mount"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-sv2c3","depends_on_id":"bd-sv1b2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

-{"id":"bd-mexte","title":"add mounted-path E2E and log-contract coverage for ext4 EXT4_IOC_MOVE_EXT","description":"Survey finding: the repo now has FUSE-side `EXT4_IOC_MOVE_EXT` parsing/validation and an open backend bead (`bd-pjcz6`), but no operator-visible mounted-path proof that the ioctl actually succeeds and preserves data semantics once the backend is implemented.

Scope:
- Add real mounted-path coverage in `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` (or equivalent) for successful `EXT4_IOC_MOVE_EXT` execution after `bd-pjcz6` lands.
- Verify moved-length semantics, post-move file contents, donor/source extent state, and failure paths that should surface stable errno values.
- Assert structured logging fields for success and rejection cases (`operation_id`, `scenario_id`, `outcome`, and error class where applicable).

Acceptance:
- There is at least one mounted-path success case and one mounted-path rejection case for `EXT4_IOC_MOVE_EXT`.
- E2E assertions prove no silent data loss or incorrect moved-length reporting.
- Logging coverage makes ioctl outcomes auditable in CI and operator troubleshooting.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","ext4","fuse","ioctl","logging"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-mexte","depends_on_id":"bd-pjcz6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-mmplg","title":"add CLI and runtime log-contract coverage for ext4 MMP refusal states","description":"Survey finding: ext4 MMP read-side validation is implemented and there is already an open conformance bead (`bd-h4vn9`) for active-MMP write-open rejection, but the operator-facing surface still lacks explicit CLI/runtime log-contract coverage for fsck/active/unsafe MMP refusal paths.

Scope:
- Add focused tests that open or mount ext4 images with `MMP` in `Clean`, `Fsck`, `Active`, and `UnsafeUnknown` states.
- Verify user-visible error messages and errno mappings remain stable for write-participating flows.
- Assert structured tracing fields for refusal paths, including status class and sequence when present.
- Keep clean-state behavior unchanged.

Acceptance:
- The operator-facing mount/open path has deterministic coverage for each non-clean MMP state.
- Structured logs surface enough detail for incident/debug use without raw block dumps.
- The new coverage complements, rather than duplicates, the lower-level conformance bead.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ext4","logging","mount","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-mmplg","depends_on_id":"bd-h4vn9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:31:17.412740808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
